data.title: Is BYD (SEHK:1211) Attractively Priced After Recent Share Price Weakness And EV Headlines
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether BYD at HK$96.75 is a bargain or a value trap, you are not alone. A closer look at how its price lines up with fundamentals can help you frame that question.
  • The stock has returned 2.2% over the last 7 days, but is sitting on a 2.4% decline over 30 days, a 2.0% decline year to date and a 23.9% decline over 1 year, compared with gains of 46.0% over 3 years and 62.2% over 5 years.
  • Recent headlines around BYD have focused on its position in the electric vehicle and battery space and how it is competing in key markets, which often feeds into changing expectations about future cash flows and risks. At the same time, investors have been watching developments in global EV demand and policies, as these can quickly affect sentiment around companies like BYD.
  • On our framework, BYD scores 2 out of 6 on valuation checks. You can see that breakdown in detail in our valuation score. Next we look at how different methods such as DCF, multiples and asset based metrics compare, before finishing with a way of thinking about value that goes a step beyond any single model.

BYD scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: BYD Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A DCF model takes estimates of the cash BYD could generate in the future, then discounts those cash flows back to today to arrive at an estimated value per share. It is essentially asking what those future CN¥ cash flows are worth in today’s money.

For BYD, the latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of about CN¥29.1b, so the model assumes conditions differ going forward. Analysts provide explicit free cash flow estimates for the early years. Simply Wall St then extrapolates further out using a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. By 2028, projected free cash flow is CN¥96.6b, and the full set of projections runs through 2035, with each year discounted back to present value.

Adding those discounted cash flows together gives an estimated intrinsic value of HK$185.12 per share, compared with the current price of HK$96.75. That gap implies the shares trade at a 47.7% discount to this DCF estimate, which is a sizeable margin.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests BYD is undervalued by 47.7%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 226 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for BYD.

Approach 2: BYD Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand because it links what you pay for each share directly to the earnings that support it. It effectively tells you how many years of current earnings the market is willing to pay for.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth potential and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually calls for a lower one.

BYD currently trades on a P/E of 20.26x, compared with the Auto industry average of 18.58x and a peer average of 7.99x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for BYD is 15.46x, which it estimates using a model that considers earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and company specific risks.

This Fair Ratio can be more informative than a simple peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for BYD’s own characteristics rather than assuming all Auto stocks should trade at similar multiples.

Comparing BYD’s current P/E of 20.26x with the Fair Ratio of 15.46x points to the shares trading above that model based estimate.

Result: OVERVALUED

On Simply Wall St, Narratives sit inside the Community page and give you an easy tool to link BYD’s business story to a forecast and then to a Fair Value that you can compare with today’s share price.

Because Narratives on the platform are updated when new information like news or earnings arrives, your story and Fair Value can shift as the facts change rather than staying fixed to a one off model output.

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today’s value.

data.description:

  • The stock closed at US$401.87, with returns of a 1.5% decline over 7 days, 13.2% decline over 30 days, 5.2% decline year to date, 32.5% decline over 1 year, 21.0% decline over 3 years and 9.6% decline over 5 years. In that context, it may be useful to step back and look at the valuation through several different lenses rather than reacting only to the chart.

Approach 1: Chemed Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of the cash a business may generate in the future and discounts those amounts back to today to arrive at an estimate of what the entire company could be worth now.

For Chemed, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is $329.0 million. Analysts provide explicit forecasts through 2027, with Free Cash Flow of $379.1 million in that year. Simply Wall St then extrapolates cash flows out to 2035, with projected figures such as $326.2 million in 2026 and $476.1 million in 2035, all in $.

When those projected cash flows are discounted back to today, the DCF model produces an estimated intrinsic value of about $717.69 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $401.87, this implies the stock is about 44.0% undervalued under these assumptions.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Chemed Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand because it links what you pay for each share to the earnings that business is currently generating. It helps you see how many dollars of price you are paying for each dollar of earnings.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth prospects and risk. Higher growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually call for a lower one.

Chemed currently trades on a P/E of 20.86x, compared with the Healthcare industry average of about 22.07x and a peer group average of 33.75x. Simply Wall St also calculates a “Fair Ratio” for Chemed of 24.51x. This is a proprietary estimate of the P/E the company might attract given factors such as its earnings growth, industry, profit margins, market cap and risk profile.

Because the Fair Ratio adjusts for these fundamentals, it can be more useful than a simple industry or peer comparison. With Chemed’s actual P/E below the Fair Ratio, this framework points to the shares trading at a discount on this metric.
